docs.title: Winston HPC User Guide
docs.text: help@winston:~      ▘    ▗   ▌▌▌▌▛▌▛▘▜▘▛▌▛▌   ▚▚▘▌▌▌▄▌▐▖▙▌▌▌   LSE Methodology's HPC for running CPU/GPU workloads via Slurm.   Use the navigation on the left to get started.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
1. Get your username and host/IP from the admin.
  2. Connect via SSH (see Getting started → Accessing Winston).
  3. Store code and configs in /home, working data in /scratch, shared datasets in /data (see Reference → Cluster resources).
  4. Run small tests on login, submit real work via Slurm.
  5. Use Software → Miniconda or Software → Spack to install tools.

New users

If you’re not sure where to start: read Getting started → Accessing Winston, then Slurm jobs → Overview. If you don’t yet have an account, email Tom at t.robinson7@lse.ac.uk.

Host details

For security, the Winston host/IP is shared privately during account setup.

docs.title: Fair usage
docs.text:
• Do not run intensive workloads on the login node (small tests are fine).
  • Use Slurm for anything requiring >2 cores or >4GB RAM.
  • Prefer short interactive sessions for debugging; submit long runs as batch jobs.

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Your home directory lives on the root drive, which is shared with the OS. Keeping it lean is important for system stability.

  • Store code, configs, and environments in /home.
  • Store large datasets, model outputs, and caches in /scratch.
  • Use /data/shared/ for datasets multiple users need.
  • See Reference → Cluster resources for quotas and directory details.

Avoid filling your home directory

Large Slurm log files, HuggingFace model caches, and forgotten data copies are the most common causes of home directory bloat.

docs.title: Direct Slurm output to scratch
docs.text:
By default, Slurm writes .out and .err files to the directory you submitted from. Always direct output to scratch:

#SBATCH --output=/scratch/%u/%x_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=/scratch/%u/%x_%j.err

%u = username, %x = job name, %j = job ID.

docs.title: Symlink large caches to scratch
docs.text:
If you use HuggingFace, Ollama, or other tools that cache large model files, move the cache to scratch and symlink:

# Move existing cache
mv ~/.cache /scratch/$USER/cache
ln -s /scratch/$USER/cache ~/.cache

# Or set environment variables (add to ~/.bashrc)
export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/hf_cache
export TRANSFORMERS_CACHE=/scratch/$USER/hf_cache

docs.title: Keep conda environments lean
docs.text:
Conda environments can grow to tens of GB. Periodically:

  1. Export environment specs so they can be recreated: conda env export > environment.yml
  2. Clean unused packages: conda clean --all
  3. Remove environments you no longer use: conda env remove -n <name>

docs.title: Software installs
docs.text:
• Keep Spack builds under /data/spack/users/$USER.
  • Do not modify anything under /opt/spack.
  • Remove unused Conda environments occasionally.
  • Export environments for reproducibility:
  • conda env export > env.yml
  • spack find --format '{name}@{version}' > spack-env.txt

docs.title: Time limits
docs.text:
• Always set #SBATCH --time realistically.
  • Jobs exceeding walltime will be terminated.

docs.title: Accessing Winston
docs.text: Winston is accessible via SSH from within the LSE network.

docs.title: SSH from within the LSE network
docs.text:
ssh <username>@<host-provided-by-admin>

Note

Replace <username> and <host-provided-by-admin> with the details provided when your account was created. If you were given an SSH key, add -i /path/to/key to the command.

docs.title: Access from outside LSE (VPN)
docs.text: To access Winston off-campus, connect to the LSE VPN and use SSH as normal.

docs.title: Transferring files
docs.text: You can move data to/from Winston using either GUI tools or command-line tools.

docs.title: GUI option
docs.text:
A tool like Cyberduck (SFTP) can transfer files to your user space on Winston using:

  • your username
  • your authentication method (password or key)
  • the Winston host (provided privately)

docs.title: Command line: scp
docs.text:
Copy local → Winston:

scp -r <path/to/local/files> <username>@<host-provided-by-admin>:<remote/path>

Copy Winston → local (reverse direction):

scp -r <username>@<host-provided-by-admin>:<remote/path> <path/to/local/folder>

Notes: - -r recursively copies directories. - Use absolute paths to avoid surprises (e.g. /home/<username>/project or /scratch/<username>/data).

docs.title: Command line: rsync (recommended for lots of files)
docs.text:
rsync is faster for large transfers and incremental updates (only changed files are sent).

rsync -av <path/to/local/files> <username>@<host-provided-by-admin>:<remote/path>

Reverse direction (Winston → local):

rsync -av <username>@<host-provided-by-admin>:<remote/path> <path/to/local/folder>

Common flags: - -a preserve metadata (permissions, timestamps) - -v verbose output - add -z to compress during transfer (useful over slower links) - trailing slash matters: data/ copies contents; data copies the folder

docs.title: Make a shortcut host entry
docs.text:
If you don’t want to type the IP every time, add a host alias to your ssh config:

nano ~/.ssh/config

Then add:

Host winston
    HostName <host-provided-by-admin>
    User <username>
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
    ServerAliveInterval 60
    ServerAliveCountMax 2

Then connect with:

ssh winston

Note

Remove IdentityFile if you are using password-based login.

docs.title: Slurm essentials
docs.text:
squeue # see running/queued jobs
squeue -u $USER # see your jobs
sbatch script.sh # submit job
salloc --mem=10G --cpus-per-task=4 --time=01:00:00 # reserve interactive resources
scancel <JOBID> # cancel job
sinfo # view partitions / nodes
sacct -j <JOBID> # view job history

Node details:

scontrol show node <node> # see node description
scontrol show job <JOBID> # see job details

Spack essentials:

spack search <name>
spack install <pkg>
spack load <pkg>
spack find
spack unload <pkg>

Conda essentials:

conda create -n myenv python=3.11
conda activate myenv
conda deactivate

docs.title: Storage & quotas
docs.text:
du -sh ~/*/ 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -10    # home directory breakdown
du -sh ~/.[^.]* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -5  # hidden directories (often large)
du -sh /scratch/$USER                              # scratch usage
quota -s                                           # check your quota status

docs.title: Compute
docs.text:
• ~192 vCPUs (188 allocatable)
  • ~250GB RAM
  • 2 × RTX 6000 Ada Generation (48GB VRAM each)

docs.title: Memory defaults
docs.text: By default, Slurm jobs are assigned 1GB RAM per CPU core unless you request more.

docs.title: Checking availability
docs.text:
winfo

Note

winfo is a local helper; you can also use sinfo and squeue for Slurm-wide views.

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Winston has two NVMe drives:

Drive Capacity Mount Purpose Micron 2048GB 1.9TB / (root) OS, home directories, /data Samsung 1024GB 954GB /scratch Working data, active job data

docs.title: Directory structure
docs.text:
/home/<username>/          Code, configs, environments (persistent)
/scratch/<username>/       Large files, active working data (persistent)
/scratch/<username>/job_N/ Auto-created per Slurm job, auto-deleted on completion
/data/shared/              Shared datasets (read/write for all hpc-users)
/data/projects/<name>/     Private or collaborative project directories

docs.title: Where to put what
docs.text: Location Use for Avoid /home/<username>/ Source code, scripts, conda environments, configs, small files Large datasets, model checkpoints, HuggingFace/Ollama caches /scratch/<username>/ Large datasets, model outputs, cache directories (symlinked from ~/.cache) — /scratch/<username>/job_N/ Throwaway intermediate files during a job Anything you need after the job ends /data/shared/ Datasets multiple users need Personal files /data/projects/<name>/ Persistent project data shared across collaborators —

docs.title: Storage quotas
docs.text:
Quotas are a safety net to prevent accidental disk-filling incidents, not a usage target.

Root drive (/) — includes /home and /data:

Role Hard cap Faculty 500GB Postdocs 500GB PhD students 500GB

Scratch drive (/scratch):

Scope Hard cap Per user 400GB

If you hit your hard cap you cannot write new files until you free space. Contact the admin if your cap needs adjusting.

docs.title: Home directory warnings
docs.text: Usage warnings are displayed at login when your home directory exceeds 50GB, 100GB, or 200GB.

docs.title: Scratch stale file notices
docs.text: Files on /scratch untouched for 6+ months will trigger a login reminder asking you to archive or remove them. Nothing is automatically deleted.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Home directories are private (other users cannot read your files).
  • Scratch directories are private (mode 700).
  • Per-job scratch is private (mode 700, owned by the job submitter).
  • /data/shared/ is accessible to all hpc-users group members.
  • /data/projects/ directories have per-project access control.

docs.title: Batch jobs
docs.text: Batch jobs are submitted with sbatch. You describe what you need (time, memory, CPUs), and Slurm schedules it.

docs.title: Example: CPU batch job
docs.text:
Create a script, e.g. job.sh:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=my_python_job
#SBATCH --output=/scratch/%u/%x_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=/scratch/%u/%x_%j.err
#SBATCH --time=01:00:00        # Max walltime (hh:mm:ss)
#SBATCH --mem=10G              # Memory per job
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4      # Number of CPU cores
#SBATCH --partition=winston    # Default partition (optional)

python my_script.py

Submit it:

sbatch job.sh

Check status:

squeue

Walltime default

If you do not set --time, the current default is 12 hours. Jobs that exceed their walltime will be terminated by the scheduler.

Memory requests

--mem is total memory for the job. If you prefer per-core memory, use --mem-per-cpu.

Direct output to scratch

Always write Slurm output to /scratch/%u/ instead of your home directory. Large log files can fill your home directory unexpectedly. %u = your username, %x = job name, %j = job ID.

docs.title: Per-job scratch directory
docs.text:
Every Slurm job automatically gets a private temporary directory at /scratch/$USER/job_$SLURM_JOB_ID. It is created before the job starts and deleted when the job ends. Use it for throwaway intermediate files.

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=my_job
#SBATCH --output=/scratch/%u/job_%j.out

JOB_SCRATCH="/scratch/$USER/job_$SLURM_JOB_ID"

# Copy input data to fast per-job scratch
cp /data/shared/my_dataset.csv "$JOB_SCRATCH/"
cd "$JOB_SCRATCH"

# ... run your computation ...

# Copy results to persistent storage before the job ends
cp results.csv /scratch/$USER/my_project/

Warning

Files left only in the per-job scratch directory are lost when the job finishes. Always copy final results to your persistent scratch or home directory.

docs.title: GPU jobs
docs.text: Use the GPU partition for CUDA workloads.

docs.title: Partitions
docs.text:
• winston (default): general CPU queue (use for most jobs)
  • winston-gpu: for GPU jobs (CUDA) and privileged access to GPU resources

GPU partition limits

The winston-gpu partition has 4 dedicated CPUs and 50GB exclusive RAM. Additional resources may be available depending on cluster load, but are not guaranteed.

docs.title: Example: GPU batch job
docs.text:
Create a batch script my_gpu_job.sh:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=my_gpu_job
#SBATCH --partition=winston-gpu
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00
#SBATCH --mem=20G
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --output=/scratch/%u/%x_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=/scratch/%u/%x_%j.err

python train.py

Submit with:

sbatch my_gpu_job.sh

Tip

Once running, use scontrol show job <JOBID> or nvidia-smi (on the node) to confirm GPU allocation.

docs.title: Interactive jobs
docs.text: Interactive jobs let you reserve resources and work “live” on a compute node.

docs.title: Request an interactive allocation
docs.text:
salloc --mem=10G --cpus-per-task=4 --time=01:00:00

Once allocated, run your commands as normal (on the allocated node).

Tip

If you need interactive work frequently, consider using tmux so sessions survive disconnects.

docs.title: One-shot interactive shell
docs.text:
You can also request a shell in one command:

srun --pty --mem=10G --cpus-per-task=4 --time=01:00:00 bash

docs.title: Slurm overview
docs.text:
Winston uses Slurm to queue jobs and allocate compute resources fairly.

You can use Winston in two main ways:

  1. Batch jobs (preferred): submit a script; Slurm runs it when resources are available.
  2. Interactive jobs: reserve resources, then work on a compute node.

Do not run heavy work on the login node

Small tests are fine, but intensive workloads must run via Slurm. Login nodes are limited to 2 CPU cores and 4GB RAM.

docs.title: Quick commands
docs.text:
squeue               # view all jobs
squeue -u $USER      # view your jobs
scancel <JOBID>      # cancel a job
sinfo               # view partitions / nodes
winfo               # view allocated resources / cluster usage (local helper)

docs.title: Partitions and priority
docs.text:
• Partitions: winston (CPU) and winston-gpu (GPU).
  • QoS priority order: faculty → postdocs → PhD students.

docs.title: Miniconda
docs.text: Miniconda is recommended for user-level environments (Python, R packages, data-science tools).

docs.title: First-time install
docs.text:
wget https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
bash Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -b -p $HOME/miniconda3
source ~/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
conda config --set auto_activate_base false
conda config --add channels conda-forge
conda config --set channel_priority strict

docs.title: Shell setup
docs.text:
Add to ~/.bashrc:

if [ -f "$HOME/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" ]; then
    source "$HOME/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh"
fi

Reload your bash profile:

source ~/.bashrc

Create and use an environment:

conda create -n py311 python=3.11 -y
conda activate py311
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib

Deactivate:

conda deactivate

docs.title: Storage tips
docs.text:
Conda environments can grow large

Environments at ~/miniconda3/ can reach tens of GB. To keep your home directory lean:

  • Clean unused packages: conda clean --all
  • Export env specs so you can recreate them: conda env export > environment.yml
  • Remove old environments: conda env remove -n <name>

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
Export a full environment:

conda env export > env.yml

Export only packages you explicitly installed:

conda env export --from-history > env.yml

docs.title: Software management overview
docs.text:
Winston is configured for user-managed software installations. You do not need administrator privileges (sudo) to install your own tools.

Two supported approaches:

  • Spack: compiled system software (R, compilers, MPI, scientific libraries)
  • Miniconda: user-level environments (Python/R packages, data science stacks)

Rule of thumb: - Use Spack for system-level or compiled dependencies. - Use Conda for Python/R packages and analysis workflows. - It’s fine to mix them (see Using envs in Slurm).

docs.title: System overview
docs.text: Layer Tool Purpose Shared base layer Spack (/opt/spack) Core system software (R, compilers, MPI, libraries) User build area /data/spack/users/$USER Your Spack-built software install tree User environments Miniconda (~/miniconda3) Python/R environments + packages Scheduler Slurm Runs jobs on compute nodes

docs.title: Using Spack/Conda inside Slurm jobs
docs.text: You can use Spack and Conda together in the same job.

docs.title: Example batch script
docs.text:
job.sbatch:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=test
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --output=/scratch/%u/%x_%j.out

# Set up Spack + Conda
source /opt/spack/share/spack/setup-env.sh
source ~/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh

conda activate py311
spack load r

Rscript myscript.R

Submit:

sbatch job.sbatch

Tip

Spack is great for optimized compiled libraries; Conda is great for high-level packages. If conda activate fails inside a job, make sure you sourced conda.sh as shown above.

docs.title: Spack
docs.text: Spack manages compiled system-level software such as R, compilers, MPI, and scientific libraries.

docs.title: Shell setup
docs.text:
Add to your ~/.bashrc (if not already present):

# Load Spack
if [ -f /opt/spack/share/spack/setup-env.sh ]; then
    source /opt/spack/share/spack/setup-env.sh
fi

Reload your shell:

source ~/.bashrc

docs.title: Installing and loading packages
docs.text:
Example:

spack install r@4.4.1
spack load r@4.4.1
R --version

Notes: - Builds go under: /data/spack/users/$USER/opt/spack - Remove availability in your shell with: spack unload <pkg> - List installed packages: spack find

Note

If a package already exists on the system, Spack may use a prebuilt binary automatically.
